response:
## Confirming the drift

The scan output shows crime_00011 (`action_label_drift`) and crime_00012 (`copy_ia_drift`) firing on the same "delete" group with identical evidence:

- `archive` — `SeatList.tsx`, `TeamList.tsx`
- `delete` — `TeamList.tsx`, `UserList.tsx`
- `remove` — `SeatList.tsx`, `UserList.tsx`

Each component uses **two of the three** verbs, so no single file is the right canonical reference — every file is part of the problem.

## Proposed canonical label set

Three verbs, three distinct semantics — the fix isn't "pick one verb" but "give each verb a job":

| Verb | Use when | Reversible? |
|---|---|---|
| **Delete** | Permanently destroys the entity and its data | No |
| **Remove** | Detaches the entity from a parent collection (the entity itself may still exist elsewhere) | Yes (re-add) |
| **Archive** | Soft-deletes — entity hidden but recoverable, audit trail preserved | Yes (unarchive) |

Mapped onto the three components:

- `SeatList.tsx` → **Remove** (a seat is a slot on a subscription; you don't destroy data, you free a license)
- `UserList.tsx` → **Delete** (the destructive case — wipe the user record)
- `TeamList.tsx` → **Archive** (teams carry historical state worth preserving)

## Which to refactor first

**`UserList.tsx`.** Reasons:
1. It's the only component where "Delete" is the *correct* canonical verb under the proposal — fixing it locks down the most-loaded word and stops the other two from drifting back.
2. It currently uses both `delete` and `remove`, which is the most user-dangerous ambiguity ("Remove" sounds reversible; "Delete" doesn't).
3. Once `UserList` is unambiguously "Delete," `SeatList` and `TeamList` can stop borrowing from that semantic space.

Order: `UserList` (lock Delete) → `SeatList` (lock Remove) → `TeamList` (lock Archive). Then add a one-line copy guide so an agent extending a fourth list component has a discoverable rule.

## How the two detectors differ on this fixture

They share the same `analyse()` core (`packages/core/src/detectors/action-label-drift.ts:64`). The only behavioural difference is one flag:

- **`action_label_drift`** runs across the whole IA index — JSX labels *plus* nav entries, button attributes, and other string-valued props the index already touches.
- **`copy_ia_drift`** passes `restrictToJsxLabel: true` (walk.ts plumbs this through), so it only sees label signals whose `kind` starts with `jsx_` — visible UI copy only, no nav/attribute fallback.

On this fixture all three verbs live inside JSX text in the List components, so the JSX-restricted view and the broader view produce identical hits, identical anchors, identical scores. The redundancy isn't a bug in the run — it's the predictable case where a frontend-only signal happens to cover everything the broader detector would catch. If `nav.ts` or a route guard string had used one of these verbs, `action_label_drift` would have picked it up and `copy_ia_drift` would have stayed silent.

Practically: tie the **fix** to `action_label_drift` (broader scope, same canonical guidance) and treat `copy_ia_drift` as the confirming UI-only witness. Both findings point at the same `pick_canonical_label` action.
